SUMMARY:
**This post is the first in a four-part series on Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs), providing an overview of the technology, use cases, privacy risks, and proposed recommendations for promoting privacy and mitigating risks associated with BCIs.** *   BCIs are computer-based systems that directly record, process, or analyze brain-specific neurodata and translate these data into outputs that can be used as visualizations or aggregates for interpretation and reporting purposes and/or as commands to control external interfaces, influence behaviors or modulate neural activity. Non-invasive neurotechnologies should not be equated to non-harmful technologies—just because a device is not directly implanted to sit on or within the brain does not mean that it does not pose unique health and other privacy and data use risks.

Brain-Computer Interfaces & Data Protection: Understanding the Technology and Data Flows
========================================================================================

**![Image 2](https://lh5.googleusercontent.com/xUiC8uyRmgyY8ZmyQ5ybw9cnCw9_ohbTlvrjlzQUcJrRGM0is_tOBEUDcnsfGrFJMJiIK0XFXi1v50xzweCjHvGFLhTVVruchy68q-z9tV24ve3d3WECT1l_E2bHTzpfRuyUmbCa)**

**This post is the first in a four-part series on Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs), providing an overview of the technology, use cases, privacy risks, and proposed recommendations for promoting privacy and mitigating risks associated with BCIs.**

_Click_[_here_](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/FPF-BCI-Report-Final.pdf)_for FPF and IBM’s full report: Privacy and the Connected Mind. Additionally, FPF-curated resources, including policy & regulatory documents, academic papers, thought pieces, and technical analyses regarding brain-computer interfaces are_[_here_](https://sites.google.com/fpf.org/future-of-privacy-forum-bci/bci-resources)_._

### **I. Introduction – What are BCIs and Where are They Used?**

Today, [Brain-Computer Interfaces](http://learn.neurotechedu.com/introtobci/#references) (BCIs) are primarily used in the health-care context for purposes including rehabilitation, diagnosis, symptom management, and accessibility. While BCI technologies are not yet widely adopted in the consumer space, there is increasing interest and proliferation of new direct-to-consumer neurotechnologies from gaming to education. It is important to understand how these technologies use data to provide services to individuals and institutions, as well as how the emergence of such technologies across sectors can create privacy risks. As organizations work to build BCIs while mitigating privacy risks, it is paramount for policymakers, consumers, and other stakeholders to understand the state of the technology today and associated neurodata and its flows.

*   BCIs are computer-based systems that directly record, process, or analyze brain-specific neurodata and translate these data into outputs that can be used as visualizations or aggregates for interpretation and reporting purposes and/or as commands to control external interfaces, influence behaviors or modulate neural activity.

*   BCIs can be broadly divided into three categories: 1) those that record brain activity; 2) those that modulate brain activity; and 3) those that do both, also called bi-directional BCIs (BBCIs).

*   BCIs can be invasive or non-invasive and employ a number of techniques for collecting neurodata and modulating neural signals.

*   Neurodata is data generated by the nervous system, which consists of the electrical activities between neurons or proxies of this activity.

*   Personal neurodata is neurodata that is reasonably linkable to an individual.

BCIs that record brain activity are more commonly used in the healthcare, gaming, and military contexts. Modulating BCIs are typically found in the healthcare context, such as when used to treat [Parkinson’s disease](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ana.23951) and other movement disorders by using deep brain stimulation. BCIs [cannot at present or in the near future](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3497935/) “read a person’s complete thoughts,” serve as an accurate lie detector, or pump information directly into the brain.

### **II. BCIs Can Be Invasive or Non-Invasive. Both Employ a Number of Techniques for Recording Neurodata and Modulating Neural Signals**

**Invasive BCIs** are installed directly into—or on top of—the wearer’s brain through a surgical procedure. Today, invasive BCIs are used in the health context for a variety of purposes, such as improving patients’ [motor skills](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0959438813002249). Invasive BCI implants can involve a number of different types of implants. An electrode array called a [Utah Array](http://learn.neurotechedu.com/introtobci/#references) is installed into the brain and relies on a series of small metal spikes set within a small square implant to record or modulate brain signals. Other prominent examples of invasive BCIs rely on electrocorticography ([ECoG](http://learn.neurotechedu.com/introtobci/#ecog)), where electrodes are attached to the brain’s exposed surface to measure the cerebral cortex’s electrical activity. ECoG is most widely used to help medical providers locate the brain area that is the center of [epileptic seizures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/electrocorticography).

Unlike invasive BCIs, **non-invasive BCIs** do not require surgery. Instead, non-invasive BCIs rely on external electrodes and other sensors to collect and modulate neural signals. One of the most prominent examples of a non-invasive BCI technology is an electroencephalogram ([EEG](https://imotions.com/blog/what-is-eeg/))—a method for recording the brain’s electrical activity, with electrodes placed on the scalp’s surface to measure neurons’ activity. [EEG-based BCIs](https://analyticsindiamag.com/10-times-companies-made-inexpensive-consumer-based-bci-devices-using-eeg/) are common in gaming where collected brain signals are used to control in-game characters and select in-game items. Another noteworthy non-invasive method is near-infrared spectroscopy ([fNIRS](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4309034/)), which measures proxies of brain activity via changes in blood flow to certain regions, specifically changes in oxygenated and deoxygenated hemoglobin concentration using near-infrared light. fNIRS is especially prominent in wellness and medical BCIs, such as those [used to control prosthetic limbs](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4309034/).

Other non-invasive techniques go beyond simply recording neurodata by also modulating the brain. For example, transcranial direct current stimulation ([tDCS](https://neuromodec.org/what-is-transcranial-direct-current-stimulation-tdcs/)) and transcranial magnetic stimulation ([TMS](https://neuromodec.org/what-is-transcranial-magnetic-stimulation-tms/)) are both used to modulate neuroactivity. Non-invasive neurotechnologies should not be equated to non-harmful technologies—just because a device is not directly implanted to sit on or within the brain does not mean that it does not pose unique health and other privacy and data use risks.

Both invasive and non-invasive BCIs are generally characterized by [four components](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3497935/):

*   **Signal Acquisition and Digitization:** Involves sensors (e.g. EEG, fMRI, ect.) measuring neural signals. The device amplifies to levels that enable processing and sometimes filters collected signals to remove unwanted data elements, such as noise and artifacts. These signals are digitized and transferred to a computer.
*   **Feature Extraction:** As part of signal processing, applicable signals are separated from extraneous data elements, including artifacts and other undesirable elements.
*   **Feature Translation**: Signals are transformed into usable outputs.
*   **Device Output:**Translated signals can be used as visualizations for research or care, or they can be used as directed instructions, including feedforward commands utilized to operate external BCI components (e.g. external software or hardware like a robotic arm) or feedback commands which may provide afferent (conducted inward) information to the user or may directly modulate on-going neural signals.

![Image 3: screen shot 2022 01 11 at 3.42.45 pm](https://fpf.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/Screen-Shot-2022-01-11-at-3.42.45-PM-1024x444.png)
### **III. Recorded Neurodata Becomes Personal Neurodata When it is Reasonably Linkable to an Individual**

Neurodata is data generated by the nervous system, which consists of the electrical activities between neurons or proxies of this activity. Neurodata can be both directly recorded from the brain—in the case of BCIs—or indirectly recorded from an individual’s spinal cord, muscles, or peripheral nerves.

At times, neurodata can be personally identifiable when reasonably linkable to an individual or when combined with other identifying data associated with an individual, such as when part of a particular user profile. The recording and processing of personal neurodata can produce information related to an individual’s biology and cognitive state that is [directly tied to that user](https://www.nature.com/articles/ncomms9885)’s record, use, or account. Additionally, the processing of personal neurodata can lead to inferences about an individual’s moods, intentions, and various physiological characteristics, such as arousal. Machine learning (ML) sometimes plays a role as a tool for helping determine if a neurodata pattern matches a general identifier or particular class or physiological state. Although identifying an individual based solely on their recorded personal neurodata is difficult, such identification has been shown to be possible with relatively minimal data (less than 30 seconds-worth of electrical activity) within a [lab setting](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-021-25895-8.pdf). [Some experts](https://voicesofvr.com/987-the-neuroscience-of-neuromotor-interfaces-privacy-implications-with-facebook-reality-labs-thomas-reardon-2/) believe that such identification is feasible more broadly in the near term.

Personal neurodata can reveal seemingly innocuous data; record behavioral interactive activity; include health information associated with an individual; or potentially provide insight into an individual’s feelings or intentions. BCIs may eventually progress into new arenas, recording increasingly sensitive personal neurodata, leading to intimate inferences about individuals. Those applications may seek to include transcribing a wide-range of a wearer’s thoughts into text, serving as an accurate lie detector, and even implanting information directly into the brain. However, these speculative uses are still in the [early research phases](https://www.newscientist.com/article/2238946-mind-reading-ai-turns-thoughts-into-words-using-a-brain-implant/) and could be decades from fruition, or perhaps never emerge.

### **IV. Conclusion**

As BCIs evolve and are more commercially available across numerous sectors, it is paramount to understand the unique risks such technologies pose. Although our report, and this blog series, primarily focus on the privacy concerns—including questions about the transparency, control, security, and accuracy of data— around the existing and emerging BCI capabilities, these technologies also raise important technical considerations and ethical implications, related to, for example fairness, justice, human rights, and personal dignity. We will highlight where additional ethical and technical concerns emerge in various use cases and applications of BCIs throughout this series.

**Read the next blog in the series:**[Brain-Computer Interfaces & Data Protection in Healthcare: Data Flows, Risks, and Regulations](https://fpf.org/blog/bcis-data-protection-in-healthcare-data-flows-risks-and-regulations/)

--- SOURCE 3: Using BCI + AI for Brain Data Acquisition and Interpretation ---
URL: https://medium.com/@opemipo404/using-ai-and-bci-to-capture-and-interpret-information-from-the-brain-3bd9c7ee0b5d

SUMMARY:
Using BCI + AI for Brain Data Acquisition and Interpretation | by Opemipo Oduntan | Medium For example, a CNN trained to classify EEG data into different cognitive states or tasks could be used to monitor brain activity in real-time or to develop BCI-based applications such as brain-controlled prosthetics or assistive technology. Moreover, a CNN trained to detect patterns in EEG data corresponding to specific actions or commands could be used to develop BCI-based applications that allow users to control devices with their thoughts, such as brain-computer interfaces for communication or rehabilitation. The power to turn the numbers perceived by BCI devices into something meaningful is left to deep-learning algorithms like CNNs. In addition to their potential to improve our understanding of the brain and how it works, BCIs also have the potential to have a range of applications in fields such as healthcare, education, and entertainment.

A rtificial intelligence (AI) and brain-computer interfaces (BCI) are rapidly advancing technologies that have the potential to revolutionize how we capture and interpret information from the brain. BCIs allow us to connect our brains to computers and other devices directly, while with AI, computers can analyze and interpret data in ways that were previously unimaginable. Together, these technologies offer the possibility of unlocking new insights into the brain’s inner workings and enabling us to better understand the way we think and feel.

This article explores the current state of AI and BCI technologies and discuss their potential applications in neuroscience and psychology. It also considers the ethical and societal implications of these technologies and the potential risks and benefits of using AI and BCI to capture and interpret information from the brain.

The following is a highlight of the topics covered in this article:

*   Details on _Brain Computer Interfaces_: **how BCIs work; different techniques;**and some **challenges/limitations of the technology**
*   Details on _Artificial Intelligence_: **definition of****AI;****how AI works;** and **modern applications of AI.**
*   How AI interprets data captured by BCI.
*   Existing applications of BCI + AI in **medicine and healthcare;****security**and **authentication;****neuromarketing**and **advertisement;****challenges/flaws;** etc.
*   Future directions for **research and development** in BCI + AI.

Before diving head first into the nuances of AI and it’s applications in BCI, let’s first understand what a BCI is and _how_ it functions.

Brain Computer Interfaces
=========================

> A **brain computer interface (BCI)**is a system that determines **functional intent** — the desire to change, move, control, or interact with something in your environment — directly from your **brain activity**. In other words, BCIs allow you to control an application or a device using **only your mind**.
> 
> 
> University of Calgary

BCIs use sensors to measure brainwave patterns and translate them into digital signals that computers can understand. These signals enable control and communication with electronic devices and, in some cases, other people.

There are four major components to a BCI:

![Image 4](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:638/1*zZfoUB85RA9dUa91cr_t3g.png)

[Block diagram of BCI system | Download Scientific Diagram (researchgate.net)](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Block-diagram-of-BCI-system_fig1_340106984) | Fig. 1

*   **Signal/Data acquisition** — records the brain waves and sends them to the preprocessing component for signal enhancement and noise reduction.
*   **Signal/Data preprocessing** — transforms raw data into a format that is more suitable for analysis and interpretation by other devices.
*   **Feature extraction**— _raw neural data,_ recorded from BCI; includes a broad set of information about brain activity and some noise from external interference.
*   **Classification**— interprets and analyzes data collected from the brain; machine learning (ML) algorithm that is designed to identify patterns in data and classify it into different categories.

This article focuses on _signal acquisition_ and _interpretation_.

Signal Acquisition
------------------

Brain-generated oscillations, also known as _brain waves_, play a crucial role in any functional brain-computer interface (BCI) system. These oscillations reflect the _voluntary neural activity_ that occurs in the brain due to a person’s current activity. Various signal acquisition methods have been developed and studied to measure brain waves. The method most suitable for a specific BCI application and its intended users depends on the measured activity and the system’s specific needs.

There are two main methods for acquiring brain activity: _invasive_ and _non-invasive_. Invasive methods involve inserting sensors or electrodes directly into the brain tissue. In contrast, non-invasive methods use external sensors to measure brain activity from the scalp’s surface.

![Image 5](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:484/0*al3o8NiKVIo1yyXZ.jpg)

[Signal acquisition methods. | Download Scientific Diagram (researchgate.net)](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Signal-acquisition-methods_fig4_282296371) | Fig. 2

Invasive Methods
----------------

Some of the most common methods of invasive signal acquisition include the cortical surface method (also known as electrocorticography or ECoG), which involves placing electrodes on the surface of the brain, and the intracortical method, which involves inserting electrodes directly into the brain tissue. Both of these techniques offer a high spatial and temporal resolution of brain activity but may carry some risks and ethical concerns.

Invasive methods have the following pros and cons:

Pros:

*   The higher spatial and temporal _resolution_ of brain activity, due to the close proximity of the sensors to the brain tissue
*   Greater _sensitivity_ to small changes in brain activity
*   Ability to record from _specific_ brain regions or structures

Cons:

*   Invasive procedures may carry risks such as infection, bleeding, or tissue damage
*   Long-term implantation of electrodes may cause scarring or inflammation
*   Electrodes may drift or become less sensitive over time
*   Ethical concerns may be raised in some cases

Non-Invasive Methods
--------------------

Non-invasive methods for capturing brain signals, such as electroencephalography (EEG), magneto-encephalography (MEG), functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), and functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), are frequently used in brain-computer interface (BCI) research. These methods use external sensors to measure brain activity from the scalp’s surface, providing a safe and non-invasive alternative to invasive techniques. However, non-invasive methods generally have lower spatial and temporal resolution than invasive methods and may be sensitive to movement or other extraneous factors that can interfere with the signal.

Electroencephalography (EEG) is a valuable tool for measuring the brain’s electrical activity with high temporal resolution — it can detect rapid changes in brain activity on a millisecond scale. It is also relatively inexpensive and portable, making it a widely used and accessible tool for brain research and general clinical practice.

During an EEG, small metal discs called electrodes are placed on the scalp, typically in a grid-like pattern. The electrodes are connected to an EEG machine by wires, which amplify and record the electrical signals from the brain. These signals are typically measured in microvolts (µV) and are displayed on a monitor as a graph, known as an EEG trace.

EEG is useful for measuring brain waves. It has a high temporal resolution, allowing for detecting rapid changes in brain activity on a millisecond scale. It is also generally inexpensive and portable, making it popular for brain research and clinical practice.

Here is a brief overview of some pros and cons of invasive and non-invasive signal acquisition methods in BCI devices.

Non-invasive methods have the following pros and cons:

Pros:

*   Safe and non-invasive procedures with minimal risks of _complications_
*   High level of patient _comfort_
*   Ability to record brain activity from a large number of locations _simultaneously_
*   More practical and convenient for _long-term_ use

Cons:

*   The lower spatial and temporal resolution of brain activity compared to invasive methods
*   Less sensitivity to small changes in brain activity
*   Some non-invasive methods may be sensitive to movement or other extraneous factors that can interfere with the signal
*   Some non-invasive methods may require the use of a conductive gel or other substances to improve signal quality

Now, with knowledge of the different signal acquisition methods, let us look at how these signals are put into a form that can be analyzed using ML and AI.

Types of Brain Waves (Electrical Signals)
-----------------------------------------

There are two main approaches to studying BCI electrical signals: _Evoked Potentials_ (EP) &_Event Related De-synchronization/Synchronization_(ERD/ERS).

EPs are changes in brain activity that occur in response to a specific stimulus, such as a sound, a flash of light, or a touch. These changes can be measured using techniques like EEG or MEG, providing valuable insights into brain processes.

There are different types of evoked potentials, including but not limited to the following:

*   **Sensory evoked potentials** (SEPs), which are measured in response to a sensory stimulus such as a sound or light
*   **Motor evoked potentials** (MEPs), which are measured in response to a motor stimulus such as a muscle contraction or movement
*   **Cognitive evoked potentials** (CEPs), which are measured in response to a cognitive task or mental activity

ERD/ERS refers to changes in brain activity that occur in response to a specific event or task. These changes can be measured using techniques such as electroencephalography (EEG) or magnetoencephalography (MEG) and are typically quantified as changes in the power of specific frequency bands in the EEG signal.

ERD is characterized by a decrease in power in specific frequency bands and is typically observed in the alpha and beta frequency ranges (8–13 Hz and 13–30 Hz, respectively). It is thought to reflect an increase in neural activity and excitability and is often associated with attention, arousal, and the preparation or execution of a task.

ERS is characterized by an increase in power in specific frequency bands, typically in the theta and gamma ranges (4–8 Hz and 30–80 Hz, respectively). It is thought to reflect a decrease in neural activity and inhibition and is often associated with relaxation, sleep, and the processing of sensory information.

Now that we have a basic understanding of the technology behind BCIs, particularly the methods used to acquire brain signals, we can explore how AI and ML fit into this field. BCIs rely on advanced algorithms and techniques to interpret brain activity and translate it into meaningful commands or messages, and AI and ML play crucial roles in this process. Using AI and ML techniques to analyze and classify brain signals, BCIs can enable users to control devices or communicate with others using their thoughts alone. In the next section, we will delve further into the role of AI and ML in BCI technology.

Artificial Intelligence
=======================

Before we get into the science and applications of AI in BCI, let’s begin by understanding _what_ exactly AI is and _how_ it functions.

> **Artificial Intelligence** (AI), a term coined by emeritus Stanford Professor John McCarthy in 1955, was defined by him as “the science and engineering of
> 
> making **intelligent machines**”. Much research has humans program machines to **behave** in a clever way, like playing chess, but, today, we emphasize machines that can **learn**, at least somewhat **like human** beings do.
> 
> 
> Stanford University

Artificial intelligence (AI) refers to the ability of machines and computer systems to perform tasks that generally require human-like intelligence, such as understanding language, recognizing patterns, learning from experience, and making decisions. AI systems can be designed to perform a wide range of tasks, from simple ones like sorting and organizing data to more complex ones like playing games, recognizing faces, or driving a car.

The following are examples of different types of AI:

*   **Rule-based AI:** This type of AI follows a set of predetermined rules or instructions to perform a specific task. It is capable of making simple decisions based on these rules but is not able to adapt or learn from experience.
*   **Machine learning (ML):** This type of AI involves training a computer system to recognize patterns and make predictions or decisions based on data inputs. Machine learning algorithms can be trained on large datasets and can improve their performance over time as they learn from new data.
*   **Deep learning (DL):** This type of AI involves using artificial neural networks, which are inspired by the structure and function of the human brain, to recognize patterns and make decisions. Deep learning algorithms are particularly good at processing large amounts of data and can be used for tasks such as image and speech recognition.

Within the different categories of AI, various algorithms and models function according to each type of AI’s specific rules and characteristics. A small collection of DL and ML algorithms make up the core of BCI technology. One of the most common deep learning models used in BCI research is the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

CNNs are a popular deep-learning model often used in BCI research. CNNs are particularly well-suited for processing and analyzing data with latent spatial dependencies, such as fMRI images or spontaneous EEG signals. They can learn and extract features from these signals by applying filters and transformations, which allows them to recognize patterns and make predictions about the underlying brain activity.

CNNs are particularly well-suited for processing and analyzing data with spatial dependencies, such as images or signals from brain activity. They are made up of multiple layers of interconnected “neurons” inspired by the structure and function of neurons in the human brain.

The key features of CNNs that allow them to process and classify data effectively are:

*   **Convolutional layers:** These layers apply a series of filters to the input data, which are used to detect and extract features from the data. The filters are typically small and move across the input data in a sliding window fashion, applying mathematical transformations to the data as they go.
*   **Pooling layers:** These layers reduce the spatial resolution of the input data by aggregating the output of the convolutional layers in some way. This can help to reduce the complexity of the model and improve its generalization ability.
*   **Activation functions:** These functions are used to introduce non-linearity into the model and allow it to learn more complex patterns in the data.
*   **Fully connected layers:** These layers connect all the neurons in the previous layers and use them to classify the input data into one or more categories.

The essential mathematical operation that underlies any given CNN is convolution. According to data engineer Pratik Choudhari, Convolution involves applying a filter or kernel to the input data to detect and extract features (key attributes in data). The convolution operation can be expressed mathematically as:

![Image 6](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*N1_yHDEALwnTwQn9wo3GXg.png)

convolutional operator

*   **output(x, y)** ⇒ output of the convolution at position (x, y)
*   **input(x + i, y + j)** ⇒ input data at position (x + i, y + j)
*   **kernel(i, j)** ⇒ weight of the filter at position (i, j)

The convolution operation is typically applied to the input data in a sliding window fashion, moving the filter across the input data and applying the above equation at each position. The convolution output is a new array of values, known as the feature map, which contains information about the features detected by the filter in the input data.

In a CNN, multiple convolutional layers are typically stacked on top of each other, with each layer applying different filters to the input data. The output of each layer is typically passed through a nonlinear activation function, such as a _sigmoid_ or _ReLU_ function, before being fed into the next layer.

After the convolutional layers, the CNN may also include pooling layers, which reduce the input data’s spatial resolution by aggregating the convolutional layers’ output in some way. This can help reduce the model’s complexity and improve its generalization ability.

Finally, the CNN can have one or more fully connected layers. These layers connect all the neurons in the previous layers and use them to classify the input data into one or more categories. The fully connected layers in a CNN calculate the output of the CNN by using a weighted sum of the production of the previous layers, followed by an activation function.

During training, CNN is presented with a large dataset of labelled examples. It uses an optimization algorithm to adjust the weights and biases of the neurons to minimize the error between the predicted and actual labels. Once trained, the CNN can classify new, unseen examples by applying the learned filters and transformations to the input data and using the fully connected layers to make a prediction. It can also be used to process and analyze new EEG data. This typically involves preprocessing the EEG data to extract relevant features and patterns and then feeding the data into the CNN. The CNN will then apply its learned filters and weights to the input data to classify it or detect patterns in it, producing an output that can be used to perform various tasks.

For example, a CNN trained to classify EEG data into different cognitive states or tasks could be used to monitor brain activity in real-time or to develop BCI-based applications such as brain-controlled prosthetics or assistive technology. A CNN trained to detect patterns in EEG data corresponding to different emotions, or mental states could be used to develop BCI-based applications that respond to the user’s cognitive state, such as personalized training programs or adaptive user interfaces. Moreover, a CNN trained to detect patterns in EEG data corresponding to specific actions or commands could be used to develop BCI-based applications that allow users to control devices with their thoughts, such as brain-computer interfaces for communication or rehabilitation.

CNNs have succeeded in various BCI applications, including the classification of brain signals for communication, control, and rehabilitation purposes.

Applications of BCI + AI
========================

Now that we’ve understood the role that AI plays in BCIs, let’s dive into some of the current applications of BCI + AI in the different modern industries we run today.

Medical and Healthcare
----------------------

The healthcare field has numerous potential applications for BCIs that could be used to take advantage of brain signals across all phases of healthcare, including prevention, detection, diagnosis, rehabilitation, and restoration. BCIs could help identify early signs of brain disorders or injuries, monitor the effectiveness of treatments, or assist with rehabilitation and recovery. Some specific examples of how BCIs could be used in healthcare include:

*   **Prevention:** BCIs could be used to monitor brain activity and identify early warning signs of brain disorders or injuries, allowing for early intervention and prevention of further damage.
*   **Detection:** BCIs could be used to diagnose brain disorders or injuries by analyzing brain activity patterns and comparing them to known patterns for specific conditions.
*   **Diagnosis:** BCIs could be used to provide more detailed and accurate diagnoses of brain disorders or injuries by analyzing brain activity patterns in greater detail and with higher resolution than other methods.
*   **Rehabilitation:** BCIs could be used to help individuals with brain disorders or injuries regain lost function or improve their quality of life by providing targeted brain stimulation or by helping to guide and monitor rehabilitation therapies.
*   **Restoration:** BCIs could be used to restore lost function or capabilities by providing direct brain-controlled input or output to devices or systems, such as prosthetics or assistive technology.

Overall, BCIs have the potential to transform the healthcare field by providing a more detailed and accurate understanding of brain function and disorders, and by enabling more targeted and effective treatments and therapies.

Neuromarketing and Advertisement
--------------------------------

The marketing field has also been an interest for BCI researchers. BCIs provide a valuable tool for understanding and improving the effectiveness of advertising by directly measuring brain activity and cognitive processes. Research has shown the benefits of using EEG evaluation in political and commercial advertisements. BCI assessments can measure the attention generated while watching activities such as TV advertisements.

Researchers in neuromarketing have also studied the effect of other cognitive functions on the effectiveness of advertising, including the ability to remember TV advertisements. By using BCIs to estimate the memorization of TV ads, they have developed a new method for evaluating advertising. This approach allows researchers to identify factors that influence attention and memory and to optimize advertising content and delivery to enhance its impact.

Other Applications of BCI + AI
------------------------------

*   **Security and Authentication:** Security systems can be vulnerable to simple passwords, shoulder surfing, theft, and _cancelable_ biometrics. _Using_ _biosignals_ like brain signals, cognitive biometrics or electrophysiology can solve these vulnerabilities. Biosignals are challenging to acquire and _synthesize_, making them resistant to spoofing attacks. They can also be valuable for disabled patients or users who lack the associated physical trait. EEG can be used as a biometric modality to send a covert warning when an authorized user is under external forcing conditions. The use of electrophysiology in security systems can improve their security and reliability.
*   **Neuroergonomics and Smart Environments:** In neuroergonomics, BCIs can be used to monitor and analyze brain activity in order to optimize the design of work environments and tasks. In smart environments, BCIs can be used to enable users to interact with and control their environment using their thoughts.

Understanding the Challenges
============================

With the power and advantages that come with the use of BCI + AI also comes the challenges of implementing this kind of tech. There are two major groups that we can break these challenges down into _technical_ challenges &_usability_ challenges. Technical challenges have to do with the system obstacles (ex., EEG features/characteristics). _Usability challenges_ are limitations that can affect how readily people will accept and use the technology.

Usability Challenges
--------------------

The training process is the first primary source of friction for end consumers when using BCI products. The user must be taught how to work with the BCI and control their brain feedback signals so that the BCI device can successfully interpret them. Training users is time-consuming.

Simplicity is essential when developing new technology, as it increases usability and user acceptance. People are likelier to use technology that is easy to understand and use and may be hesitant to adopt or effectively use complex or challenging technology. It is essential to prioritize simplicity in its development to ensure the successful democratization of BCI + AI technology.

Technical Challenges
--------------------

Technical challenges also present difficulties for researchers working to advance the field of BCI. These challenges can hinder progress and make developing new technologies and applications more difficult.

Several technical issues related to the recorded electrophysiological properties of brain signals can impact the development and implementation of BCI technologies. These issues include non-linearity, non-stationarity, and noise in the brain signals, as well as the challenges of working with small training sets and the accompanying dimensionality curse. These issues can make it challenging to interpret and analyze brain signals accurately and can impact the performance and reliability of BCI technologies.

Now, with an understanding of the boundaries that exist within the field of BCI + AI, let’s being to understand what the future holds for the research and development of BCI + AI.

Future of BCI + AI
==================

In the near future, it is anticipated that research and development within BCI + AI will primarily focus on optimizing these technologies’ accuracy, reliability, and usability. This will involve the ongoing development of new algorithms and models capable of effectively interpreting brain signals, as well as the creation of robust BCI systems. Additionally, researchers will likely continue to explore the potential applications of BCI + AI in various other fields, such as education, self-regulation, and even games and entertainment.

Research may also be directed toward developing new hardware and software technologies further to advance the capabilities of BCI + AI systems. This could include the creation of advanced sensors and processors and the design of software tools and frameworks that facilitate the development and deployment of BCI + AI systems. In summary, future research and development in the fields of BCI + AI is expected to encompass a wide range of efforts to improve the performance and functionality of these technologies and find new ways to apply them across various industries. Evidence of this is already being seen with companies like Neurable, NeuraLink, and OpenBCI pioneering in BCI as leading developers of tech that may be democratized and readily available in the future.

So What?
========

BCIs are a promising technology that has the potential to fundamentally change the way we interact with computers and other devices. They can substantially advance our understanding of the brain; thus, AI, the cornerstone of BCI technology, is an essential skill in this modern age. The development and use of BCIs is a revolutionary and foundational field of research, with the potential to impact many areas and industries and change the world. With the democratization of BCI technology through startups, the relevance of BCIs in modern society will only grow.

To summarize, the application of AI in BCI has the potential to revolutionize the way we interact with computers and other devices. BCIs allow users to control devices and perform tasks using their thoughts or brain activity, providing a more intuitive and natural way for people to interact with technology. Abstract
--------

The brain-computer interface (BCI) technology has emerged as a groundbreaking innovation with profound implications across diverse domains, particularly in health care. By establishing a direct communication pathway between the human brain and external devices, BCI systems offer unprecedented opportunities for diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation, thereby reshaping the landscape of medical practice. However, despite its immense potential, the widespread adoption of BCI technology in clinical settings faces several challenges. These include the need for robust signal acquisition and processing techniques and optimizing user training and adaptation. Overcoming these challenges is crucial to unleashing the complete potential of BCI technology in health care and realizing its promise of personalized, patient-centric care. This review work underscores the transformative potential of BCI technology in revolutionizing medical practice. This paper offers a comprehensive analysis of medical-oriented BCI applications by exploring the various uses of BCI technology and its potential to transform patient care.

**Keywords:**_Alzheimer_, _brain-computer interface_, _human-computer interaction_, _neurofeedback_, _prosthetic control_

Introduction
------------

The brain–computer interface (BCI) technology has emerged as a pioneering innovation with profound implications across various domains, particularly in the realm of health care. By bridging the gap between the human brain and external devices, BCI systems offer innovative solutions for diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation, thereby transforming the landscape of medical practice. BCI holds significant importance and has made substantial impacts across multiple fields, particularly in recent years. BCI technology has revolutionized numerous domains across various fields by facilitating direct communication between the human brain and external devices.[[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R1)] Especially in health care, BCI has transformed diagnostics, treatment, and rehabilitation processes, offering personalized and targeted solutions for patients with neurological conditions and physical disabilities.[[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R2)] Furthermore, numerous other applications are found in fields such as gaming, education, and human-computer interaction (HCI), enhancing user experiences and fostering innovation.

In this review paper, we present an extensive analysis of BCI applications, with a primary focus on its pivotal role in medical settings. Through this comprehensive analysis of relevant research papers, we aim to explore the diverse applications of BCI technology and its potential to revolutionize patient care. Moreover, BCI applications in medical practice are further segmented into distinct categories, facilitating a systematic exploration of its multifaceted impacts on the healthcare industry. The taxonomy adopted in this paper includes Accessibility, General Medical Applications, Psychology/Neurology, Pediatric Applications, and Personalized Medicine.

For this review work, PubMed was selected as the primary database due to its extensive collection of biomedical and life sciences literature, which is highly relevant to medical applications of BCI technology. By leveraging PubMed, the study ensures access to peer-reviewed articles pertinent to BCI and multimodal emotion recognition in healthcare contexts. Supplementary databases such as Scopus, Web of Science, and IEEE Xplore were considered to broaden the scope, integrating perspectives from allied health and engineering. The literature review covered studies published from 2014 to 2024, capturing advancements in BCI and emotion recognition over the past decade. This timeframe was chosen to include the most recent developments in the field while allowing historical context to be considered.

The taxonomy is presented in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#jmss_49_24-f001), including the number of papers reviewed in each section. The taxonomy outlines the distribution of reviewed papers across five major categories, with each category thoroughly explored. As illustrated in [Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#jmss_49_24-f002), it ensures a comprehensive examination of the medical applications of BCI technology. The systematic review process employed the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) model, which is pivotal for ensuring transparency and reproducibility in research.[[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R3)] This model was chosen for its structured framework that enhances the clarity and comprehensiveness of systematic reviews. The significance of the PRISMA model lies in its ability to guide researchers through each stage of the review process, from protocol development to reporting, thus minimizing bias and improving the overall quality of evidence synthesis.[[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R4)] Following the PRISMA method, this taxonomy initially involved collecting 300 papers covering different BCI applications. From this pool, we refined our focus to 220 papers specifically related to BCI in medical applications. Notably, this process involved filtering out combined applications beyond the medical scope, focusing solely on medical applications, both invasive and noninvasive. Some examples of searching methods or strategies using keywords and Boolean phrases in PubMed are as follows: “Multimodal emotion recognition,” “brain–computer interface,” “EEG emotion recognition,” “machine learning” “Prosthetic control,” “brain–computer interface applications.” These search terms ensured the retrieval of articles spanning the interdisciplinary approaches necessary for studying BCI applications in health care.

Brain–computer Interface System
-------------------------------

The human brain is referred to as the most sophisticated organ, captivating the curiosity of researchers, scholars, and engineers for centuries. The capabilities and intricacies of the human brain is a fascinating and interesting source of exploration, pushing the boundaries of technology and neuroscience. One of the most notable achievements in this area is the development of the BCI system, which builds a remarkable connection between the human brain and computers or machines. BCI system represents an advanced merging of computer science, neuroscience and engineering while offering the potential scopes to interact with the human brain or mindset with technology. This technique empowers individuals with several disabilities, such as lost motor functionalities and paralysis

At its core, BCI technology is sophisticatedly connected with capturing and interpreting psychological signals, such as electroencephalogram (EEG). These signals serve as a gateway to unlock the remarkable potential of establishing a direct connection between the human mind and external devices. BCI leverages the electrical activities generated by the brain (EEG) while decoding emotions, thoughts, and intentions and translating the signals into meaningful instructions for machines, computers, and prosthetic devices. It is crucial to explore the relationship between the physiological signals and technology interfaces to understand the extraordinary capabilities of the advancement of BCI systems. The overall process of integrating the BCI system for medical sector application with EEG signals is illustrated in [Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#jmss_49_24-f003).

### Integration of brain–computer interface system with physiological signals EEG for medical applications

The process of integrating BCI technology with psychological signals can be outlined as follows:

1.   Signal acquisition: This process begins with the signal acquisition from EEG or ECG experiment from the electrodes placed on the scalp (for EEG). The electrical activities of the brain and heart are represented by the signals[[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R5),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R6)]

2.   Preprocessing: The extracted signals are then preprocessed to remove artifacts, noise, and any unwanted interferences.[[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R7)] This is a crucial step that ensures that the data is clean enough and suitable for further analysis

3.   Extracting features: Relevant, significant features, including amplitude value, components, frequency, or other key characteristics, are later extracted from these preprocessed signals, which carry insightful information about the users’ condition or state[[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R8)]

4.   Recognizing patterns: Machine learning, deep learning algorithms, or other pattern recognition techniques are employed to analyze the extracted features. These algorithms undergo training to identify and recognize signatures or patterns within the data that correspond to physiological or mental states[[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R9)]

5.   Medical intervention: The BCI system makes determinations based on the patterns related to the user’s intentions or states.[[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R10)] As an illustration, it can determine whether the user wants to manipulate a cursor on a particular screen or initiate a command.

    1.   Controlling command generation: The BCI system converts the decision into a control command for an external application or device. Such command generation encompasses a wide range of potential actions, such as controlling robotic arms, computer cursors, wheelchairs, or any other devices or software that users intend to interact with

    2.   External device’s output: The generated control commands are transmitted to the external devices or applications, which subsequently carry out the desired actions or responses to the user’s intent

    3.   End: The BCI system continues looping through these stages, allowing real-time interaction between users and external devices/applications. The process operates continuously and adaptively, with the BCI system constantly updating its understanding of the user’s intentions.

Applications of Brain–computer Interface
----------------------------------------

The scope and applications of BCI are versatile within and beyond the medical sphere. In the medical realm, it has been providing hope and transformative remedies for individuals with profound disabilities, facilitating the regain of both mobility and communication skills. BCI systems are playing promising roles in assisting patients in recovering from neurological diseases and ailments.[[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R11)] There are numerous research works where scholars have worked on developing such systems. To gain deeper insights and understanding into the breadth of this research, it can be categorized into distinct taxonomies or segments.

BCI medical applications are diverse and promising, ranging from advancing mobility to enhancing patient accessibility. Numerous transformative solutions can improve the lives of numerous patients facing many complicated health issues.[[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R12)] From the perspective of modern real-world applications, BCI technology has demonstrated immense significance in assistive technology while providing support to people with disabilities. BCI-controlled prosthetic devices, such as exoskeletons and limbs, enable the disabled to regain their motor functions and independence. Moreover, neuroplasticity and motor learning are other ground-breaking methods that provide real-time feedback to patients with spinal cord injuries, stroke, and other neurological conditions. The modern applications of such technology in medical domains are diverse, and this medical application domain can be further thoughtfully divided into distinct categories to comprehend the vast scope and significance better. The categories include “Accessibility,” which encompasses prosthetic control, rehabilitation, and wheelchair mobility solutions; “General Medical Applications,” which extends to communication support and neurological condition management; and “Psychology/Neurology,” which delves into areas such as Alzheimer’s disease detection, depression assessment, emotion classification, epilepsy monitoring, and stress evaluation. Each of these segments plays an important role in harnessing the potential of BCI technology to enhance healthcare outcomes, from restoring mobility and communication abilities to advancing our understanding of complex neurological and psychological conditions.[[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R13)]

### Accessibility

The objective of such studies is to research BCIs on patients suffering from amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), brain stroke, brain/spinal cord injury (SCI), cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, and so forth. BCI accessibility applications represent a revolutionary advancement while improving the lives of individuals with physical limitations and communication challenges.[[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R14)] One specific application in this regard is showcased by Manyakov _et al_.[[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R15)] where noninvasive BCIs are used based on electroencephalograms (EEG) recorded on the subject’s scalp, requiring no surgical procedure. The event-related potentials (ERPs) that were the focus of this study were electrophysiological responses to an internal or external stimulus using the P300 BCI response. The study was conducted using the prototype of a miniature EEG recording device that communicates wirelessly with a USB stick receiver. This accessibility domain can be further segmented into prosthetic control, rehabilitation, and wheelchair mobility solutions.

#### Prosthetic control

There are various techniques for controlling a prosthetic hand, including a shoulder harness, myo-electric control, and the WILMER elbow. A shoulder harness requires movement of the upper arm or shoulder, while myoelectric control requires some nerves or muscle activity in the amputated extremity.[[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R16)] The WILMER elbow uses elbow motion to control the hand. However, these systems are not useful for patients with total paralysis, but an EEG-based BCI provides a new control channel for individuals with severe motor impairments. This involves detecting motor actions from the EEG to control an externally powered prosthesis device during grasping. The BCI can be controlled by a binary output signal obtained through the classification of EEG patterns during hand movement imagination. Utilizing oscillatory EEG components as input signals for a BCI necessitates real-time analysis of EEG signals.

Guger _et al_.[[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R17)] combined recent BCI developments with modern prosthetic tools. The BCI experiment involved the use of EEG to control a prosthesis through binary output signals, obtained by classifying EEG patterns during imagination of left- and right-hand movements. The EEG setup consisted of a minimum of two electrodes, positioned close to primary hand areas (C3 and C4), to capture oscillatory EEG components as input signals for the BCI. In another study, Miranda _et al_.[[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R18)] used Blackrock Microsystems NeuroPort data acquisition system for recording prosthetic data. It was also used in converting neural firing rates into functional mapping. For the prosthetic limb commands in endpoint velocity space, mathematical models were integrated into BCI systems to restore and/or facilitate near-natural neural and behavioral functions to advance neural decoder capabilities through multi-scale, dynamic models for the brain’s plastic changes.

Furthermore, Katyal _et al_.[[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R19)] developed a method for collaborating with the BCI approach for the autonomous control of a prosthetic limb system, enabling amputees to achieve more natural, efficient, and intuitive control of their prosthetic limbs. The project proposes a BCI system that uses a combination of electroencephalography (EEG) and electromyography (EMG) signals to facilitate the control of a prosthetic limb. The system uses a deep neural network to classify and interpret the EEG and EMG signals, which are then used to control the prosthetic limb in real-time. Similarly, Laiwalla _et al_.[[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R20)] proposed a distributed wireless network of sub-mm cortical microstimulators for BCIs. This system aims to enhance the functionality and performance of BCIs by enabling precise and targeted neural stimulation, thereby improving the control and feedback provided to the user.

In another study, Chapin _et al_.[[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R21)] used a linear decoder to map the recorded neural activity to the desired movement of the robotic arm in real-time. The decoding algorithm was implemented in MATLAB software and incorporated a Kalman filter to estimate the state of the arm and correct for errors in the decoding process. The authors report high accuracy and low latency of the neural interface in controlling the robotic arm. The study suggests the potential of the proposed neural interface for the development of advanced prosthetics for individuals with motor disabilities. Again, Oppus _et al_.[[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R22)] described the design and development of a 3D-printed prosthetic hand that incorporates sensors for BCI control and a voice recognition module for voice commands. The authors reported successful testing of the prosthetic hand on a single user. This demonstrates the potential of this technology to improve the life quality of patients with upper limb amputations.

In the research works,[[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R23),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R24),[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R25)] authors attempted to implement machine learning based predictive modeling for decoding ERPs and understand the intend of robotic arms. A combination of EEG and EMG is also presented here to demonstrate that the use of such a BCI could improve the performance of a user’s control over a robotic arm. The results of the study from Aly _et al_.[[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R24)] showed that the hybrid BCI system using both EEG and EMG signals achieved an average classification accuracy of 81.9% for the grasping and releasing task. The extracted features from both EEG and EMG signals were used to train a machine learning model based on a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) to decode the user’s intended movement.

Both invasive and noninvasive EEG techniques hold immense significance in clinical settings, especially in the domain of neuroscience and medical practice. Noninvasive EEG is widely used in clinical for diagnosing and monitoring various neurological conditions such as epilepsy, sleep disorders, and brain injuries. It provides valuable insights into brain function and helps clinicians make informed decisions about treatment and management strategies. Invasive EEG, although more invasive and typically reserved for specific clinical scenarios, offers unparalleled precision and detail in recording neural activity.[[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R26)] It is crucial for neurosurgical planning, particularly in cases where precise localization of brain regions is required, such as tumor resection or epilepsy surgery. Invasive EEG also plays a significant role in research settings, where it enables scientists to investigate the underlying mechanisms of brain function and develop novel treatments for neurological disorders.

While both invasive and noninvasive methods offer greater spatial resolution and signal quality, they require surgical procedures and carry associated risks. There are relatively fewer works focused on invasive EEG in this domain, indicative of the challenges and limitations associated with invasive procedures. For instance, Beyrouthy _et al_.[[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R27)] presented a study on EEG mind-controlled smart prosthetic arms, demonstrating the feasibility of utilizing invasive EEG for prosthetic control. However, the adoption of invasive EEG technologies in prosthetic applications remains limited due to the invasiveness of the procedures and the need for further research to address associated challenges.

#### Rehabilitation

BCIs have primarily been studied for the purpose of providing assistive technologies to individuals with severe motor disabilities caused by neurodegenerative diseases or strokes. The use of BCIs for enhancing motor and cognitive recovery within neurorehabilitation settings is a newly emerging field of research. While most rehabilitation tools require minimal motor control, BCIs allow patients with severe motor deficits to participate in therapeutic tasks. EEG-based paradigms include sensorimotor rhythms, slow cortical potentials, ERPs, and visually evoked potentials are commonly used in this case.[[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R28)]

Several studies have been conducted to assess cognitive functions in paralyzed ALS patients and in patients with physical disabilities due to neurological diseases.[[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R29),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R30),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R31),[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R32),[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R33)] The evaluation of cognitive abilities in patients with severe motor disabilities is a challenge and a less explored area, but some attempts have been made using ERPs. Three EEG-based modalities (SCP, SMR, and P300) are promising solutions for EEG-BCI system realization. While many studies have demonstrated successful BCI operation, others have shown low performance rates in terms of both CA and ITR.[[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12180781/#R34)] P300-BCI exhibits higher ITRs but is greatly affected by the severity of the disease, while SMR-based BCI systems are adaptive but have the disadvantage of being unreliable for some subjects. enriched_points.expanded_content: Surveys collect subjective data from participants about their experiences and perceptions regarding BCIs, helping researchers understand user attitudes, usability, and potential ethical concerns.

Experimental studies involve controlled environments where participants use BCIs to perform tasks, allowing researchers to measure cognitive responses and assess technology effectiveness with quantifiable data.
Observational methods involve monitoring BCI users in real-world settings, enabling researchers to gather insights on user interactions, challenges, and the practical impact of BCIs on daily life.
